name: Heng'e
shortDescription: Heng'e is an alternate name for Chang'e, the Chinese moon goddess, celebrated for her beauty and association with the Mid-Autumn Festival.
description: Heng'e, more commonly known as Chang'e, is a prominent figure in Chinese mythology, revered as the moon goddess. Her legend is deeply intertwined with the Mid-Autumn Festival, a significant cultural celebration. The most popular myth recounts how she consumed an elixir of immortality, either accidentally or to prevent it from falling into the wrong hands, and ascended to the moon, where she resides in the Guanghan Palace. In some versions, she is accompanied by a jade rabbit, who prepares medicine for her. Heng'e's story embodies themes of sacrifice, love, and the longing for home. Her image has evolved over centuries, reflecting changing cultural values and artistic interpretations. She is a symbol of beauty, grace, and the ethereal allure of the moon.
subtitle: The Moon Goddess of Chinese Lore
role: Moon goddess, symbol of the Mid-Autumn Festival, embodiment of beauty and grace

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The myth of Heng'e (Chang'e) has several variations. One popular version tells of her husband, the archer Hou Yi, who shot down nine of the ten suns that were scorching the earth. As a reward, he received an elixir of immortality. In some accounts, Heng'e consumed the elixir to prevent a villain from stealing it, while in others, she ingested it out of curiosity or a desire for immortality. Regardless of the reason, she floated to the moon, becoming its goddess. Another common element in the myth is the presence of a jade rabbit, often depicted as her companion, who grinds the elixir of immortality. The story is a central element in the Mid-Autumn Festival, celebrated when the moon is at its fullest and brightest.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Heng'e plays a significant role in Chinese culture, particularly during the Mid-Autumn Festival. Families gather to admire the full moon, eat mooncakes (which often depict Heng'e), and share stories about her. She represents the beauty and mystery of the moon, and her tale serves as a reminder of the importance of family, love, and sacrifice. In some regions, she is also worshipped as a fertility goddess, and prayers are offered to her for blessings of children. Her image is often used in art, literature, and advertising, further cementing her status as a beloved figure in Chinese culture.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Heng'e's story is rich in symbolism. Her ascent to the moon can be interpreted as a metaphor for transcendence and the pursuit of immortality. The moon itself symbolizes purity, tranquility, and the cyclical nature of life. The jade rabbit represents healing and longevity. The act of consuming the elixir, whether intentional or accidental, highlights the themes of choice, consequence, and the complexities of human desire. The separation between Heng'e and her husband, Hou Yi, evokes feelings of longing, loss, and the enduring power of love across vast distances. Her tale offers a poignant reflection on the human condition and the eternal quest for meaning.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Heng'e continues to be a prominent figure in modern Chinese culture. Her story is retold in countless books, films, and television shows. She is a popular subject for artists and designers, and her image is often used in advertising and branding. The Chinese lunar exploration program, Chang'e, is named after her, reflecting her enduring association with the moon. The Mid-Autumn Festival remains a vital cultural celebration, ensuring that her legend will continue to be passed down through generations. She is a powerful symbol of Chinese heritage and a testament to the enduring power of myth.

sources.author: E.T.C. Werner
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Chinese myths and legends, providing details about Chang'e's various origin stories and cultural significance.

sources.author: Suzanne Cahill
sources.work: Transcendence & Divine Passion: The Queen Mother of the West in Medieval China
sources.description: Provides insights into the broader context of Chinese mythology and the role of female deities, including Chang'e, within the religious landscape.

associations.name: The Moon
associations.significance: Heng'e is the goddess of the moon and resides there.

associations.name: Jade Rabbit
associations.significance: Heng'e's constant companion on the moon.

associations.name: Immortality
associations.significance: The elixir that transformed her into a goddess.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Selene
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Selene and Heng'e are lunar deities associated with the moon and its cycles.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Tsukuyomi
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Tsukuyomi is the Japanese god of the moon, sharing a similar celestial domain with Heng'e.

companions.name: Hou Yi
companions.description: Heng'e's husband, a skilled archer in Chinese mythology.

companions.name: Jade Rabbit
companions.description: A rabbit who lives on the moon with Heng'e and grinds the elixir of immortality.

worshipPractices.name: Moon Gazing
worshipPractices.description: Admiring the full moon during the Mid-Autumn Festival, often accompanied by offerings and prayers to Heng'e.

worshipPractices.name: Mooncake Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offering mooncakes, often decorated with images of Heng'e, to honor the goddess during the Mid-Autumn Festival.

festivals.name: Mid-Autumn Festival
festivals.timing: 15th day of the 8th month of the lunar calendar
festivals.description: A major Chinese festival celebrating the harvest and the full moon, with a focus on Heng'e and her legend.

cultural.modernLegacy: Heng'e's story continues to inspire art, literature, film, and even space exploration programs. She remains a beloved figure in Chinese culture, symbolizing beauty, grace, and the enduring power of myth.
